title: Financial Projections
READ_THIS_FIRST: This file has two layers. The first — phase spend plan, milestone-gated funding logic, and scenario structure — describes HOW to think about AquaVision finance without pretending measured traction exists. The second — assumed_financial_projection below — fills in a five-year illustrative model with dollar figures, subscriber counts, and three scenarios (pessimistic / base / optimistic). Every number in that section is tagged assumption or modeled_projection, derived from the inputs in unit-economics.json and pricing-revenue.json, not from company data, because there are zero users and zero revenue today. Use the assumed model to stress-test the plan and show sensitivity; do NOT cite it as forecast, traction, or 'our projections show'. Present the BASE case as the planning case and the pessimistic case unprompted. When Phase 1 launches, replace assumed rows with measured rows — do not amend assumptions in place without updating provenance.

what_can_honestly_be_projected.statement: Costs are estimable in all three phases. Revenue is only plausibly modelable once Phase 2 is live with real conversion data, and Phase 3 revenue remains as unknowable as in the original hardware-only plan until its own discovery work is done.
what_can_honestly_be_projected.reasoning: We know roughly what a small product team, hosting, and community operations cost (Phase 1). We do NOT yet know Phase 2's conversion rate, ARPU, or churn — see unit-economics.json — so Phase 2 revenue is a scenario, not a projection, until Phase 1 ships and generates real usage data. Phase 3 hardware costs and revenue remain exactly as unknowable as before: no BOM quotes, no price, no customer.

phase_spend_plan.note: Order-of-magnitude only. Currency in USD. These are planning envelopes, not budgets.

primary_costs: Farmer discovery interviews (Phase 1 feature validation, not just hardware WTP)
Technical architecture decisions (mobile stack, AI model approach)
Legal (app store terms, data privacy posture)
phases.spend_envelope: low — deliberately cheap, de-risks product-market direction before a line of app code is written at scale

spend_envelope_usd.note: 2-3 person founding team, discovery travel, legal — see assumed_financial_projection#/annual_opex_model

phases.unlocks: Confidence that the free-tier feature set (guide, logging, disease-ID, community) matches what beginner/smallholder farmers actually need, before committing build budget.

phases.phase: Phase 1 — Foundation (build and launch the free app)
primary_costs: AI disease-ID and farming-assistant model development (likely built on existing foundation models plus fine-tuning, not from scratch)
App store and organic-channel growth operations — deliberately NOT a paid-acquisition budget line, per the go-to-market channel assumption
phases.spend_envelope: low-medium — the cheapest phase that produces a real, used product, and the one this whole model depends on
phases.unlocks: A launched free app with measured installs, activation rate, and retention — the first real data this company will have ever had. This is the user-growth investment the phased model is named for: spend here buys users and data, not revenue.

phases.phase: Phase 2 — Monetization (layer in premium, marketplace, consultations)
phases.duration_months: 4-8, once Phase 1 shows retained, active usage
primary_costs: Consultation marketplace build (recruiting and paying aquaculturists/vets)
Conversion-funnel design and in-app growth engineering
phases.spend_envelope: medium — funded partly by early Phase 2 revenue if conversion holds, otherwise entirely investment
phases.unlocks: The first real revenue the company has ever had, and — critically — the first measured free-to-paid conversion rate, ARPU, and churn, replacing every assumption in unit-economics.json#/phase_2_monetization_economics with a real number. This is the revenue ramp gate: Phase 3 spend should not begin in earnest until this gate produces evidence the software business itself is viable.

phases.duration_months: 8-14 for a first field validation season, unchanged in shape from the original hardware-only plan
primary_costs: Drone/dock/sonde BOM and subsystem prototyping (winch, sensor payload)
Field validation and paired reference sampling
phases.spend_envelope: high — the largest and longest-lead capital outlay in the plan, exactly as it was when hardware was the whole company

spend_envelope_usd.note: Per first validation season; BOM still unquoted — upper bound is a planning ceiling, not a quote.

phases.unlocks: A validated, sellable hardware-as-a-service offering for the commercial/enterprise segment. This phase's cost structure and risks are inherited wholesale from the original single-phase hardware plan; becoming a software company first does not make the drone cheaper or the regulatory pathway faster.

phase_spend_plan.the_honest_note: Dollar envelopes above are order-of-magnitude planning bands (modeled_projection), not signed budgets. Phase 1/2 totals depend on team size and cloud/AI inference costs that Phase 0 discovery will pin down; Phase 3 totals depend on a hardware BOM that still has zero real quotes. The SHAPE — cheap product-first phases that generate their own evidence, followed by an expensive hardware phase that only makes sense once that evidence exists — is the honest and useful part. See assumed_financial_projection for a year-by-year roll-up of these envelopes.

the_milestone_gated_funding_logic.principle: Raise against de-risking milestones in each phase, not against a revenue projection — for software milestones exactly as much as for hardware milestones.
the_milestone_gated_funding_logic.why: Each phase gate removes a specific risk: Phase 1 removes 'will anyone use this for free'; Phase 2 removes 'will a viable fraction pay'; Phase 3 removes the same hardware risks the original plan named (payload closes, winch works, profiles validate, farmers pay for automated monitoring). An investor funding this is buying successive de-risking across a software business first and a hardware business second. See funding-ask.json.
the_milestone_gated_funding_logic.the_kill_gates: Phase 1 has an implicit kill gate: if activation and organic retention are poor even at zero price, the free tier is not solving a real problem and Phase 2/3 should not be funded on top of it. Phase 3 keeps its explicit kill criteria from the original plan (payload budget cannot close; commercial farmers show no response capacity or WTP for automated monitoring). A financial plan that includes the conditions under which each phase should NOT proceed is more credible than one that only projects success.

revenue_scenario_structure.status: FILLED with ASSUMED numbers in assumed_financial_projection below — replace with measured data post-launch. Phase 3 hardware revenue in that section uses a placeholder site price pending farmer discovery.
revenue_scenario_structure.phase_2_model_when_ready: Monthly recurring revenue = (active free users) x (free-to-paid conversion rate) x (ARPU, blended across subscription + marketplace + consultation) x (1 - monthly churn), compounding month over month across the growth ramp, minus Phase 1/2 cost to serve.
revenue_scenario_structure.phase_3_model_when_ready: Unchanged from the original plan: Revenue = (docks deployed) x (sites per dock) x (price per site) x (1 - churn), summed across the deployment ramp, minus cost-to-serve per dock.

the_three_scenarios_to_model_per_phase.phase_2_assumptions: Conversion well under the 2-5% generic-software benchmark, high churn, low ARPU attach beyond bare subscription.
the_three_scenarios_to_model_per_phase.phase_3_assumptions: VLOS-only economics, sparse sites, high maintenance, low price.
the_three_scenarios_to_model_per_phase.purpose: Tests whether either layer of the business should exist as currently shaped. If catastrophic, the model is too fragile.

the_three_scenarios_to_model_per_phase.phase_2_assumptions: Conversion within the modeled 2-5% band, churn and ARPU near the midpoints in unit-economics.json.
the_three_scenarios_to_model_per_phase.phase_3_assumptions: BVLOS achieved, mixed site density, measured maintenance, verifiable-value pricing.
the_three_scenarios_to_model_per_phase.purpose: The realistic planning case for each phase.

the_three_scenarios_to_model_per_phase.phase_2_assumptions: Conversion above the generic benchmark (plausible if community trust and data-compounding retention work as hoped), strong marketplace/consultation attach.
the_three_scenarios_to_model_per_phase.phase_3_assumptions: BVLOS, dense cooperative clusters, controlled maintenance, institutional contracts, and a genuine Phase 2-to-Phase 3 warm-lead effect on CAC.
the_three_scenarios_to_model_per_phase.purpose: The upside, presented as upside — never as the base case.

revenue_scenario_structure.the_discipline: When these are filled in, present the BASE case as the plan and show the pessimistic case unprompted, for both phases. A three-scenario model where the pessimistic case is survivable is far more fundable than a single-line projection that only goes up — and this is exactly as true of a freemium user-growth curve as it is of a drone-deployment curve.

assumed_financial_projection.label: Illustrative five-year model for planning and sensitivity analysis only

model_inputs.note: Every input below is borrowed from unit-economics.json and pricing-revenue.json or invented for scenario spread. None is measured for AquaVision.

mau_as_pct_of_cumulative_activated_users.note: Simplified retention funnel — not a cohort model.

assumed_site_monitoring_price_usd_month.note: Placeholder until farmer WTP discovery — NOT a committed price. Anchored below verifiable aeration-savings logic in pricing-revenue.json.

months_of_hardware_revenue_in_year_5.note: Pilot ramp — not a full deployment year.

annual_opex_model.note: All-in operating spend (team, cloud, community, field ops allocation). Excludes one-time capex write-offs beyond normal prototype spend.

pessimistic.label: Pessimistic — low conversion, high churn, no hardware revenue in horizon
pessimistic.interpretation: Software layer barely registers; Phase 3 should not be funded on this trajectory. Tests whether the company should exist as currently shaped.

base.label: Base — mid-range SaaS assumptions, small Phase 3 pilot in year 5

revenue.hardware_note: 2 docks x 6 sites x USD 350/site/mo x 6 months

end_of_horizon_run_rate.note: Still loss-making at base-case opex in 2030 — software revenue alone does not fund Phase 3 pilot without further raise.

base.interpretation: Realistic planning case: modest SaaS traction, hardware pilot late and small. Use for milestone-gated tranche sizing in funding-ask.json.

optimistic.label: Optimistic — strong conversion and community-led growth, earlier hardware attach

net_burn_usd.note: Illustrative cash-flow-positive year on aggressive conversion — treat as upside, not plan.

revenue.hardware_note: 5 docks x 10 sites x USD 500/site/mo x 9 months

five_year_net_surplus_total_usd.note: Cumulative surplus only under optimistic inputs — not the planning case.

optimistic.interpretation: Upside case if community trust, conversion, and cooperative hardware density all exceed generic benchmarks. Present as upside only.

suggested_raise_usd.note: Phase 3 pilot capex dominates; raise sized above one-year burn to cover validation season length.

how_to_use_this_section: Present base scenario as the internal planning case; show pessimistic unprompted in any investor conversation.
Replace mau_end and paying_subscribers_end with traction.json values the moment real data exists — do not let assumed rows become stale 'facts'.
Attack the conversion and ARPU assumptions first — they move revenue more than any other input.
Phase 3 hardware lines are the least trustworthy rows in the table; treat them as order-of-magnitude placeholders.

what_investors_should_take_from_this_fil.statement: The assumed_financial_projection section exists so reviewers can stress-test the plan — not so the founders can pretend traction exists. Zero users and zero revenue remain the factual starting point.
what_investors_should_take_from_this_fil.the_pitch: 'Here is our milestone-gated spend plan, here are the assumptions the model rests on — starting with a freemium conversion rate borrowed from generic benchmarks — and here is a three-scenario sensitivity model showing what happens if those assumptions are wrong. Fund Phase 1, watch us replace the assumed rows with measured ones within months, and decide the Phase 3 hardware raise from evidence.'
what_investors_should_take_from_this_fil.the_risk: Some investors want a single hockey-stick line and will pass without it. Showing pessimistic/base/optimistic with every input tagged is a filter for investors who diligence assumptions — which is the point.
